speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Rose Minsky. I'm Leslie Troper, I'm the interviewer. We're in Brooklyn, New York on December 7, 1994. Tell me about the day you were deported from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The day I was deported, I was working at in the shop, as I told you about, that did the coats. I had a sleeve in the machine. Of course, whenever I could do damage, I did. Sometimes I-- a lot of times I put in the sleeve that no soldier could put his arm in. I sewed it down here-- he couldn't put his arm in. Whatever I could do damage, I did. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was after the first uprising of the Warsaw ghetto-- in the-- in this big ghetto. They ordered everybody down. Everybody. There was on this street, a couple shops-- [INAUDIBLE] inside the shop, [INAUDIBLE] at this shop. Every shop, they were working for the Germans, the-- the things that they need for the war. We were ordered to street. When everybody was assembled, they took up to the umschlagplatz. On the way to that umschlagplatz, from where the trains went away, we-- we past the big ghetto. We-- we passed the big ghetto. And we had to-- we stepped on boiling pieces of wood, boiling bricks that fell from the-- from the uprising. Still was burning in our feet. That's how we came to the umschlagplatz. And I tell you, over there it was a terrible thing to be. That umschlagplatz, I never forget. Never forget. We were treated like-- not even like animals. Hit and sick-- sick people were laying, people with children, people were vomiting, people were-- it's horrible to even talk about it. That took us like a day and a night, till they-- because the trains left and when they were filled up already, we have to wait until we were going to unload in Treblinka or in other places, the people. And then they came back for us. They didn't have so much-- so many trains as many as the people they had to push them all in, in one time. We had to wait-- sometimes days and nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: The journey to Majdanek was about a day and a night, I think. But we stood a couple hours in the middle of a field. And they were waiting to get orders there to bring half people various place, because the crematoriums were overfilled. Day and night, they were working 24 hours. And we were waiting till they going to have room for us. Then we came to Majdanek. We had to throw everything in-- in big barrels. We had to throw in our documents, out family pictures, everything that we possess-- our jewelries, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to keep anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We had to undress and just go through naked, just like the mother had me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this where you separated from your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when they made the selection. And I was-- after the cold shower, we were-- we were given clothes. The clothes was from other dead people. So of course, they didn't fit. Long, or too short, or too small. Or I got a shoe, one shoe too big, one shoe too small.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went over-- they were standing by the wall. A lot of shoes were there on the-- on the floor. And I want at least to pick a pair of shoes that I should be able to work. So I got hit over the head from SS woman. Her name was Brigida. She has famous. They called her Brigida. She was famous of being cruel. She hit me with the back of the gun in my head, and she said to me, what do you think, you're in a shoe store? You're going to pick shoes? You have to wear what you was given.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I cannot put on. It's too small. Another-- another bang in my head. That's where they put people. The women were sometimes even worse than the men. I witnessed-- during the time I was in Majdanek, I witnessed a couple of hangings from girls that were trying to escape, young girls. We had to stay-- oh, we were thousands of women. We had to stay, and make a big circle, and watch. If I turned my, I couldn't see it, I was hit. I had to see it. They wanted me to watch, closely to see what's going to happen when I'm going to do the same thing, when I escape. After being a couple months there-- to be a couple months in Majdanek, to be even one week in Majdankek-- it was terrible. It's a terrible-- was a terrible death camp. The food was so bad that everybody developed dysentery-- the sickness. I had some kind of look that one SS woman was to me-- I don't know. She took a liking to me. In the field when they took us work, she said that she saw that I couldn't work. I was too week. She made me to lay in the grass the whole day. And I said, I'm afraid. I don't want to lay, because every hour-- another-- an SS man came riding by on a motorcycle, checking if you are not faking, if we really work. The work was to pick, in springtime, the young vegetables out of the ground. So of course, if we could, we ate it. And this, it gave us some vitamins, this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to that SS woman, I don't want to lay. I'm strong enough I can work. Because when he going to pass with that motorcycle, see me laying, I'll be right away taken to the crematorium or shot. I said-- so So she said, don't be afraid. If I'm going to hear the motorcycle make noise, I'm going to give a whistle-- she going to whistle and I'm going to make believe I'm working.

speaker: interviewee
text: She took a liking to me all of a sudden. And that's how I was saved. I didn't work that day, a-- a couple days. She let me rest. This this was-- I can to Majdanek beginning of-- beginning of May. And by September, I think, they liquidated Majdanek because the Russian army approached.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1943. They liquidated. How did they liquidate it? Half of the people they finished off with the-- took to the crematorium. Other ones, they made selections. And I fell in, in this selection, to the group that was sent to Auschwitz, to Birkenau Auschwitz. Some people were sent to other camps. My group went to Auschwitz. So they put us in, again in trains that were from coal. They were shipping coal. And we came out just black from that voyage, from Majdanek to Auschwitz. As soon as I came to Auschwitz, I came off the ramp and they-- they shaved my hair. I was all bald, no hair. And they gave me a tattoo. I have the tattoo. See, it's still a mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: My number was 468006. This was my number. In Birkenau, they gave me that number. In Majdanek, they didn't. We had numbers sewed on here, which I don't remember the number. But this, I never will forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Auschwitz, I worked in many places. The first place I worked they also called it the death kommando. Why is it death kommando? Because nobody could last longer on this, by this work than a couple days, a week mostly. We had to carry stones, bricks. We had to lay bricks to make a pavement, to make a highway.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were young girls. We had to work big on our knees and carrying those big wheel barrows with-- with those bricks and doing this kind of-- kind of work. Nobody could last long on this job. Then I was transferred to work with the shoe kommando. The shoe kommando was by victims' shoes. We said-- this was already to sit in-- in a barrack. It was not in the open air, but in a barrack with knives in our hands. We had to cut the soles and look for valuable things. And there were barrels. They were watching us-- we had to throw in the valuables in those barrels. Of course, there was a kapo-- the one who-- and she made-- befriended a German. And they split up. And they took a lot for themselves-- this kapo. Then they took me from this to [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was the most horrible place to work. Most horrible. This was the place where the transports came. I saw with my own eyes, children being taken to the crematorium. I heard the screaming. I still hear them in my ears. I had to sort-- I had to assort the clothing. And they-- when these people went through the selection, I had to-- they had to be naked. And I had to assort the clothing from one pile of children's clothes, one pile of women's clothes, one pile of men's clothes. We have to make bundles . And we had to throw them on trucks. And this went to Germany for the German population, this clothing. And we were rushed to work fast-- [GERMAN]. They were standing over us with those guns and yelling that we were too slow. We-- we're not working enough. Matter of fact, they gave us a punishment if we were too slow.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was the punishment? To work this kind of job, to work 36 hours with no food, with no drink, and with no sleep. And I don't know how we made it. Nobody fell. I still cannot realize where we got the strength to do it. We were all worn down, we all hardly could stay on our feet. And we all made it. When you were on the shoe duty, did you find valuables inside of the shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Old worn shoes-- I didn't find a thing. Maybe some other people, but I never found a thing. And if I would find, I would have to throw it in the barrel.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't do nothing with this. What could I do with this? I couldn't hide. It was too dangerous. What did you do after you were on the clothing detail?

speaker: interviewee
text: This went on-- yeah, one thing, a remarkable thing happened, in that barrack where I slept, that had the girls that did the same kind of work, I look out the window, I see a crematorium's in flames. The whole crematorium in flames. They made an uprising there, in Birkenau, and they burned the crematorium. Some girls that they worked in the ammunition factory smuggled dynamite in. Four girls, they were all perished-- they shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were 400 men working as sonderkommando-- I don't know if you heard about the sonderkommando? That's a kommando who-- who was working by the dead people. To burn the people, to take them out, to-- to burn-- to gas them, to put them into the crematorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Jews, all Jews, that they were picked from the transport-- strong, husky men. And they somehow got together with these girls. And they asked them if they can smuggle out dynamite. And they did. Of course, these men, if they finished a group, they didn't last more than a couple weeks on the job, because they didn't want them to be too long witnesses. They finished them off, and then they choose another a group to do this kind of work. So this-- they got the dynamite. And they dynamite the crematorium. It went-- they were chose already to be sent away somewhere to die, this sonderkommando-- these 400 men. So they figured if they got to die, at least they should do something. And they did. And it was burning. And the SS came in to our barrack, because our barrack was facing that crematorium. We were the nearest. They came in, the SS, with machine guns. And we thought they were going to shoot at us, because we are living witnesses. And they don't like that we should be able to tell the world what happened. This was already towards the war-- towards the end. This was already in 1945. No-- yeah-- no, it was the end of 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came in with those machine guns. But they didn't should on us. They put them through our windows and they shot all the 400 sonderkommando-- the men. They all shot them out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said my girlfriend-- I had a girlfriend over there that I lived with so like she was my adopted sister. We got very close. She lost everybody, I lost everybody. So I said, now is our next. They won't let us live through and see this. But somehow, they left us. They had already, I think, too much worries about themselves that time to remember us. Soon after this-- this was the 18th of January, already 1945, they gave us-- they ordered us to go out in the cold. It was very cold, in the snow, to stay on roll call. The whole woman's camp, and the same thing they did in the men's camp. And they took us out of Auschwitz. We had to run by foot for-- for miles. They-- they were in the motorcycles, on the motorcycles, and they out-- trucks. We had to run. And they chased us, because the Russians were near. They didn't want us to be living witnesses for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they chased us deep into Germany, where they were-- where they were far away from the-- from the front line, where the war was going on. And that's how I escaped. I escaped from that death march. How long was that death march? How many days and nights?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was maybe a week long. Whoever couldn't run, they shot in the back. There were two ravines full of dead people. Some of them I knew. They couldn't run. They didn't have no more strength. They shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. No food. They-- in the evening, they-- they were afraid that in the evening, we were going to run away, so they put us in barns, in stables, in barns. And they were watching us for the night. Soon as the light came, they chased us again. I managed to be the first one under a truck-- they-- they were a truck full of their bread, loaves of bread that they prepared for themselves. I found a piece of glass. I managed to cut the rope from that cover that covered that truck and the loaves of bread start falling out. And people were grabbing bread. This was my satisfaction that I did. Another truck was with conserved meat for them. We did this one, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody knew if was you doing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were on the back. They were chasing us, start falling out. Then we came and we hide it in our clothes-- a loaf of bread, a can of conserved meat. When we came to the stable for the night, we made us a party. We ate. [LAUGHS]stable And then, when they that it was safe from the Russians, that were quite away-- quite a distance-- they put us in open trains, open-- open cars full of snow, cold. The whole night we were riding-- we were on that train-- open train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to the-- to the other girl, to my friend, I said, where are we going now? Is it not enough after being in Majdanek and being in Auschwitz, after being in the ghetto-- which was also like a camp-- I said, to go now? I was sure that they were not taking us to live. Somewhere to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, let's try. Let's escape. The train was going very slow, because it was a lot of snow. Very slow, the snow didn't let the train go fast. So-- and it was open wagons. It was a little ladder on the other side. All we have to do, I said, step on that ladder and go down.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what we going to do next? We were talking like-- and why this thought came to me was because the gendarme who was watching us was an old Hungarian man, I think. Already an older-- not such young. He had-- was with his uniform, with his fur ship coat, with the collar stoop up, the rifle on his arm. And I noted that on his pants, the military pants, I noticed civilian pants. He was wearing civilian pants, in order that when the Russians will surround us, he throws off the uniform and he's a civilian. He don't know about nothing. And this occurred to me that why should I let them take me for my death when he's already prepared to escape? So I said, but what we going to do? We did it. We climbed up-- this was 5 o'clock in the morning. It was still dark. January the 8th, I remember the dates, in 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we climbed down. But we found ourselves in a field of snow-- with no money, with a tattoo-ation on my arm, with nowhere to go, cold and hungry. It was good I escaped-- I-- I got rid of this. But again, I can die this way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had no choice. We went with the tracks, straight with the tracks, till we found the first little hut. That was in Upper Silesia-- it's still under the Germans. What was the name of the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]-- I forgot, but it was a sign. [POLISH]-- that's in Polish. It was on the border between Poland and Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we saw the first hut appeared. We had no choice, I said. What's going happen will happen. I knocked at the door. But we made before an agreement that none of us are going to speak German, because with the German we don't speak well. And they will recognize in the accent that we're Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're never going to admit that we're Jewish girls. We are Polish girls. We came to Germany willingly, as volunteers, to work for the industry, to help the Germans, to work in the factory. We escaped the town, that I knew that I knew that the Russians were there already. We escaped the town, because we didn't want to be with the Russians. Which I was looking at the minute they should come in, but I had to make up that story in order to-- to survive. And I said to her, [NON-ENGLISH]-- you don't admit that you're Jewish. And the minute you admit you're Jewish, we're dead. And I knocked at the door. And a young woman, a German woman, opened the door for us. It was very early in the morning still. And I told her the same, the story that I just told you. I loved them very much, I came to Germany to work for them. And I-- and I left behind my whole family in Warsaw-- in Warsaw. By this time, I didn't have already nobody, no family, but I had to lie. And we went and our group, stood-- took a rest the train, we went down to look for some--a drink of water, and we missed the train. The train left without us. This was my story. And the woman starts telling us her hard luck story, that her husband is already two years on the Stalingrad fighting very-- she didn't hear from him two years. God knows if he's still alive. She started crying. In the next room, was two little kids were sleeping. She showed us, on the wall was her wedding picture. And she was friendly. Because if you were note Jewish, she was friendly. Said we were Polish girls. First of all, I said we were frozen. We were-- we are cold. So she gave us hot coffee, she gave us homemade bread. And we warmed up by a wooden stove-- wooden stove. And we warmed up. We sit by her about two hours, but she didn't want to keep us long, because she was afraid. It was wartime. We had no identification. We might be spies, so she said, that's it. We cannot be no more by her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where we go next? She said, so what shall we do? She gave us an advisement, too. We go next, not far away, there is the Gestapo, the police. We go to them and they will know what to do with us. We tell them the same story that we told her.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked out of her and I said to my friend, we have no other choice. We're going to die here in the snow and the cold. What's going to happen is we just don't use the-- the German language. Say you don't understand German and just don't-- don't admit that you're Jewish, no matter what. After all, I said, you don't have written here that we're Jews. The Polish people recognize a Jew right away, the Germans not. If you don't tell a German that we're Jewish, he won't know. Then I came to the Gestapo. We knocked at the door. And an older officer with that-- you know that swastika on his arm, opens. And I come in, and I start talking Polish, only Polish. I said, [POLISH]-- [POLISH] means good morning. He says right away to me, [GERMAN]-- Heil Hitler, I had to say. He came to our police station. I should have said Heil Hitler, not good morning. I repeated to him. On the walls, they were all Hitler's pictures-- the whole cabinet, Hitler, Goering and Goebbels-- everyone was hanging on the wall, the pictures. And-- and he-- I keep telling him the same story that I told the woman-- that we came from Warsaw, and my name is-- right away I have to think of a Polish name, so and so. Rosalia Warshvska. I didn't went too far from Rose to Rosalia, from [NON-ENGLISH] to Warshvska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warshvska? W-A-R-S-H-V-S-K-A, Warshvska.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your friend's name that you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sasha. And I still don't remember her second name. Sasha was her first name. She died. She went to Israel. She died in Israel.
